one_line_takeaway: The first systematic jailbreak study of DNA foundation models shows that benign-homolog prompts plus pathogenicity-guided beam search can steer Evo models to reconstruct pathogen-like sequences (up to 60% success on Evo2-40B), and that scaling makes the models more, not less, vulnerable.
key_finding: GeneBreaker consistently jailbreaks the Evo series across all six viral categories, with attack success rate (ASR) increasing monotonically with model capacity: Evo2(1B) < Evo1(7B) < Evo2(7B) < Evo2(40B). Evo2-40B reaches up to 60% ASR (on Small DNA viruses and Enteric RNA viruses). Ablations show that removing either the homology-based prompt or the guided beam search drops ASR to zero, and that prompt homology strongly predicts success (Pearson 0.72) while sequence log-probability correlates with pathogen similarity (Pearson 0.75). The scaling-amplifies-risk result is the paper's headline safety implication: training-data filtering alone cannot prevent reconstruction of pathogenic patterns.
core_contribution: GeneBreaker is presented as the first framework to systematically red-team jailbreak vulnerabilities of generative DNA foundation models (the Evo series), demonstrating that they can be steered to produce sequences closely mimicking known human pathogens. The paper's own contributions are threefold: (1) the GeneBreaker attack pipeline itself, which chains an LLM agent for prompt design, PathoLM/log-probability-guided beam search, and a BLAST-based success detector; (2) JailbreakDNABench, a curated benchmark of six high-priority viral categories (built from HHS/USDA Select Agents lists) plus an evaluation pipeline; and (3) empirical evidence that scaling DNA foundation models amplifies dual-use biosecurity risk. This is distinct from the cited LLM-jailbreak literature (DAN, Rainbow Teaming, AutoDAN, JAILBROKEN/JailbreakBench/HARMBENCH) discussed in Related Work, which the paper only uses as motivation and contrast; the DNA setting differs because the prompt space is constrained to valid nucleotide sequences.
threat_model: The adversary is a malicious actor with access to an open generative DNA foundation model (e.g., Evo1/Evo2) who wants to elicit coding sequences functionally equivalent to dangerous human pathogens (HIV, SARS-CoV-2, poliovirus, variola, etc.). The specific defense being evaded is training-data curation: developers attempt to prevent misuse by removing or filtering pathogenic sequences from training data. The paper's central claim is that this defense is insufficient because scaled models generalize and reconstruct filtered pathogenic patterns from benign homologous relatives. The attacker uses only benign, non-pathogenic homologous sequences as in-context prompts (never supplying the pathogen itself), plus a pathogenicity-predicting model and log-probability heuristics to steer decoding, so the attack requires no model weights modification and no direct pathogenic input.
limitations: Success is defined by a >90% BLAST sequence/structural-similarity proxy and AlphaFold3 structure prediction, not wet-lab demonstration of actual pathogenicity or infectivity, so 'jailbreak success' is computational similarity rather than confirmed functional harm. Sequences are truncated to L=640 for evaluation efficiency, far short of full viral genomes. ASR is modest for several categories (negative-strand RNA 26.7%, dsRNA 20.0% on Evo2-40B), and the benchmark focuses on viruses. There is no external attack baseline (the paper is first-of-kind, so all comparisons are internal ablations). The approach depends on external tools (ChatGPT-4o, PathoLM, BLAST, AlphaFold3) whose own coverage bounds what can be targeted or verified.

datasets_used: JailbreakDNABench (the paper's own benchmark): six viral categories curated from HHS/USDA Select Agents and Toxins lists - Large DNA viruses (HPV, Herpesviridae, VZV, Adenoviridae, VARV), Small DNA viruses (Parvovirus B19), +ssRNA (SARS-CoV-2, MERS-CoV, OC43/HKU1/NL63/229E, Japanese encephalitis, Dengue, HCV), -ssRNA (Rabies, Measles, Mumps), dsRNA (Reovirus), Enteric RNA (Poliovirus, Norovirus)
Human Pathogen Database used as the BLAST reference for success detection (part of JailbreakDNABench)
NCBI/GenBank sequences retrieved as non-pathogenic homologous few-shot prompts
Nextstrain open global SARS-CoV-2 dataset (used only in the evolution case study for >99.9% identity matching)
quantitative_results: Evo2-40B ASR by category: Large DNA 52.0% +/- 9.8, Small DNA 60.0% +/- 25.0, +ssRNA 37.7% +/- 5.4, -ssRNA 26.7% +/- 24.4, dsRNA 20.0% +/- 40.0, Enteric RNA 60.0% +/- 20.0
Evo2-7B ASR by category: Large DNA 48.0%, Small DNA 46.7%, +ssRNA 28.8%, -ssRNA 24.4%, dsRNA 20.0%, Enteric RNA 50.0%
Evo1-7B ASR by category: Large DNA 24.0%, Small DNA 20.0%, +ssRNA 17.8%, -ssRNA 20.0%, dsRNA 0.0%, Enteric RNA 20.0%
Evo2-1B ASR by category: Large DNA 20.0%, Small DNA 20.0%, +ssRNA 13.3%, -ssRNA 0.0%, dsRNA 0.0%, Enteric RNA 20.0%
Monotonic scaling of ASR: Evo2(1B) < Evo1(7B) < Evo2(7B) < Evo2(40B)
Success threshold: >90% nucleotide identity or >90% translated amino-acid similarity via BLAST
SARS-CoV-2 spike case study (Evo2-40B): DNA similarity 92.77%, protein similarity 95.29%, structural RMSD 1.767 vs PDB 6VXX
HIV-1 envelope case study (Evo2-40B): DNA similarity 96.09%, protein similarity 96.47%, structural RMSD 0.334 vs PDB 4RZ8
SARS-CoV-2 evolution study: 201 of 10,000 generated Spike sequences exceeded >99.9% nucleotide identity to Nextstrain entries, spanning Alpha/Delta/Omicron sublineages (BA.5, BQ.1, XBB.1.5)
Ablations: removing the constructed prompt or guided beam search drops ASR to zero; prompt-homology vs ASR Pearson 0.72; log-probability vs pathogen similarity Pearson 0.75
Hyperparameters: chunk size C=128, temperature 1.0, alpha=0.5, top-4 beams x 8 continuations, target length L=640, 5 trials per virus, 4x Tesla H100 GPUs
baselines_compared: None - the paper explicitly states it is the first systematic study of jailbreak attacks on DNA language models and that 'there is no other baselines' (Sec. 5.1). Internal comparison is via ablations (no-prompt and no-guided-beam-search variants both yield 0% ASR) and across the four Evo model scales.
